The term 'Organization' refers to a structured group of individuals or entities that work together to achieve a common goal. While the existence of an organization depends on the presence of its members, the property of being an organization itself is not contingent. An organization can continue to exist even if some of its members leave or are replaced. Therefore, the property of being an organization is considered Rigid. | identity: ]

The term 'Organization' refers to a structured group of individuals or entities that work together to achieve a common goal. The term 'Agent' refers to a role played by an entity (such as a person, organization, or software) when it acts on behalf of another or performs an action. Being an agent is not essential to the identity of the underlying entity; a person can exist without being an agent, and they can cease being an agent while still remaining the same individual. Since every instance of 'Agent' is an entity that could potentially exist without this property, it is classified as Anti-Rigid. | identity: ]

The term 'Agent' refers to an entity that performs actions or acts on behalf of another entity. Ontologically, an agent cannot exist in a vacuum; it is defined by its capacity for agency (acting upon something) or its representational relationship to a principal. Whether viewed as a role (acting for another) or a functional capacity (acting within an environment), its existence as an 'Agent' is extrinsically defined by its relation to the actions it performs or the entities it represents.
